top_categories.workout_context: 334 miles. Your legs will beg to quit after 120. This trains the power output you need from mile 130 onward.
15,000ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 334 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Tour of the Gila's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before April.
334 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 15,000ft of climbing on paved mountain roads through Gila National Forest does to your power file on race day.
Tour of the Gila's paved mountain roads through Gila National Forest forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 334 miles.
Tour of the Gila's paved mountain roads through Gila National Forest rewards steady threshold power. Surging and recovering costs more watts than just holding.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on paved mountain roads through Gila National Forest. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
Tempo is Tour of the Gila's race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 334 miles on paved mountain roads through Gila National Forest. Train it until it's automatic.
At Tour of the Gila, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Tour of the Gila. Train it until it feels boring.

race_overlay.nutrition: Ultra-distance fueling for 334 miles: 80–100g carbs/hour from mile 1 — don’t wait until you’re hungry. Practice your exact race-day nutrition in every long training ride. Carry backup calories. 334 miles burns 8,000–12,000+ calories — you cannot replace them all, but you must try.
race_overlay.altitude: Tour of the Gila with 15,000ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%. Altitude: racing at 5,900-7,400 ft reduces oxygen availability; High-altitude UV exposure — intense and relentless.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, VO2max, and Endurance to prepare you for 334 miles of paved mountain roads through gila national forest in Silver City, Grant County, New Mexico, USA.
